title: Dog Raw Sores Behind Ears: Causes, Care, and When to See a Vet
meta_desc: Learn about raw sores behind your dog's ears, urgency levels, and why photos help vets triage this common but painful skin condition effectively.
content_html:
Understanding Raw Sores Behind Your Dog's Ears

It can be distressing to see your pet in discomfort, especially when you notice raw, weeping, or inflamed skin behind their ears. These sores are often the result of intense scratching or head shaking caused by underlying irritation. Common culprits include ear infections (otitis), skin allergies, or ear mites. Because the skin in this area is thin and sensitive, frequent scratching can quickly lead to secondary bacterial or fungal infections that require medical intervention.

Is This an Emergency?

The urgency level for raw sores behind the ears is generally considered Medium. While usually not immediately life-threatening, these sores are very painful and can worsen rapidly without treatment. You should aim to schedule a veterinary appointment within 24 to 48 hours. If your dog is acting lethargic, has a fever, or the area is showing signs of severe swelling and pus, an urgent care visit is highly recommended.

Why Taking a Photo Helps Triage

Taking a clear, well-lit photo of the affected area is a vital step in the triage process. A photograph allows a veterinary professional to assess the severity of the inflammation, the presence of discharge, and the extent of the skin damage remotely or before the physical exam begins. This visual information helps the team prioritize your pet's case and ensures they are prepared to provide the most effective treatment plan as soon as you arrive at the clinic.

faqs.question: Can I put human antibiotic ointment on the raw sores?
faqs.answer: It is best to avoid any topical treatments until a vet has examined the area. Some human medications contain ingredients that are toxic to dogs if licked, or may cause further irritation if the eardrum is also affected.

faqs.question: How can I prevent my dog from making the sores worse?
faqs.answer: The most effective way to prevent self-trauma is to use an Elizabethan collar (cone). This stops your dog from scratching the area with their back paws, allowing the skin a chance to heal.

faqs.question: Will these sores heal on their own?
faqs.answer: Rarely. Because the sores are usually secondary to an underlying issue like an infection or allergy, the root cause must be treated by a veterinarian to stop the cycle of scratching and inflammation.
